title: Loan-slide: tax reforms hit housing
full_text:
A sharp slowdown in loan applications has heightened fears that the property market is cooling faster than expected, with the Reserve Bank warning of the impact of Labor’s controversial budget tax changes and the risk an extended downturn could slow the overall economy.
The value of dwelling approvals declined for the third consecutive month in May – a run unmatched since the peak of Covid in 2021 – while housing credit growth has slumped to its slowest pace in a year, as higher interest rates and tax changes send home prices and auction clearance rates tumbling.
New lending is expected to halve in growth over the coming year, with investment house Macquarie warning in a note to clients that the banking sector was facing a pricing war. The Commonwealth Bank and National Australia Bank have already slashed mortgage rates for investment loans by 10 to 15 basis points as major lenders fight it out for a shrinking market.
Westpac’s retail bank chief Carolyn McCann warned in June the bank was bracing for a slowdown in investor borrowing following Jim Chalmers’ changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing.
Macquarie banks analyst Victor German said: “Recent feedback from brokers suggests that new lending flows are tracking down about 20 to 30 per cent year on year. We estimate that new owner occupiers flows are down about 10 to 20 per cent and new investors down as much as about 50 per cent. This compares with Westpac’s application volumes tracking down 20 per cent year on year and NAB application values down about 15 per cent quarter on quarter.” Anthony Albanese on Wednesday strongly defended Treasury modelling that house prices would still grow – possibly at a slower rate – following his government’s changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax.
“Treasury forecasts aren’t week by week,” the Prime Minister told the ABC. “They’re serious forecasts done based upon modelling and a range of other economic modelling showing exactly the same thing. The great news is that this Saturday, like last Saturday, first home buyers would have rocked up to auctions and not been competing with investors who want to negatively gear their properties and have taxpayers backing in those investments. So, a level playing field. What we’re seeing increasingly is stories right around the country of people who had almost given up on owning their first home, getting access to a roof over their own head.” The Reserve Bank said its interest-rate-setting board had noticed the housing slowdown was occuring faster than expected due to the government’s tax changes and interest rate rises.
RBA board members noted that “conditions in the housing market had eased by more than expected, reflecting … tax changes announced in the Australian government budget,” as well as increases in the cash rate.
“Conditions in the established housing market had softened and housing credit growth looked set to slow in the period ahead,” the bank said in meeting minutes released on Tuesday.
“This reflected the pass-through of monetary policy tightening and, more recently, tax changes for housing investors announced in the Australian government budget,” the RBA said.
Capital city home values as recorded by Cotality, have fallen 1.3 per cent in the June quarter, with Sydney prices down 3.2 per cent and Melbourne values down 2.6 per cent. The RBA warned that a further slowdown in demand could hit the broader economy.
“Members also noted the risks associated with a potentially material weakening in housing markets, including if this were to inhibit growth in consumption,” the central bank said.
The latest Procore and Property Council Industry Sentiment Survey shows confidence falling to 92 index points over the June quarter, a five-year low, with all markets anticipating weak economic growth and declining construction activity.
The quarterly survey was conducted following the budget’s controversial tax changes.
Property Council of Australia chief executive Mike Zorbas said the results reflected a significant shift in investment confidence at a critical time.
“This is not good news,” Mr Zorbas said. “Industry is reporting a clear loss of momentum when we need new commercial, industrial, retail and housing projects for our growing cities more than ever.” Former Macquarie Capital executive Richard Young, who now runs home equity lender Midkey, said he expected a significant change in behaviour among property investors that would leave fewer sales as people delayed the hit from the new capital gains tax regime. He said people would likely ramp up property renovations that could further force up construction costs, but also stimulate economic activity.
“There could be a significant and nationwide behavioural shift driven by the capital gains tax and negative gearing reforms outlined in the latest federal budget,” Mr Young said.
“For decades, residential property and share market investment have been dominant wealth-creation strategies.
“However, with major policy changes impacting these on the horizon, more Australians will be rethinking how they structure their balance sheets and allocate capital going forward.” Mr Young’s company, which has settled more than $100m in loans, comprising more than 270 customers nationally, is preparing for the tax changes to lead households into re-directing capital away from investment properties and restructuring non-superannuation-related share portfolios.
“For those with strong equity positions in their homes, there could be greater interest in unlocking that capital to fund renovations that elevate their property’s value when they decide to sell, invest in business opportunities, meet rising education costs for kids at private schools, help adult children onto the property ladder, or simply improve cashflow flexibility in a higher cost-of-living environment,” Mr Young said.
